claim: The 2024 Africa Fertilizer and Soil Health Summit produced the Nairobi Declaration, an African Union declaration focused on fertilizer access, soil health, and continent-wide soil-health action planning.

source.title: Nairobi Declaration - 2024 Africa Fertilizer and Soil Health Summit

method: Reviewed the African Union document page for the 2024 Nairobi Declaration and used it only to verify the summit output and policy-planning scope.
limitations: This is a policy declaration source, not a fertilizer-use dataset.
The source does not support sub-national fertilizer-access estimates or soil-nutrient depletion rates.
Quantified fertilizer-use and agricultural-GDP-loss claims require separate statistical or peer-reviewed sources.

claim: Soil nutrient data from AfSIS and national soil surveys shows severe nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium depletion across much of SSA's cropland, with nutrient mining (removal exceeding application) documented in most smallholder farming systems.
method: Reviewed AfSIS soil data documentation, spatial coverage, nutrient-analysis methodology, and validation studies for SSA.
limitations: AfSIS coverage is concentrated at research sites and along transects — spatial interpolation between sites introduces uncertainty for areas without direct soil sampling.
Soil nutrient measurements represent a snapshot in time — nutrient depletion rates require repeated measurements that are available at very few sites.
Nutrient-balance calculations depend on crop-yield and fertilizer-application data that varies in quality across SSA countries.
The 'nutrient mining' characterization is from research-site studies — extrapolation to all smallholder systems assumes similar management practices.
